---
title: "Astronomy at the Bottom<br>of the World"
subtitle: "Part II: Observing & Living at the South Pole"
occasion: Astronomy on Tap
description: Public talk on observing the CMB from the South Pole
author:
  - name: Cail Daley
date: April 16, 2026
date-format: long

output-file: index.html

format:
  revealjs:
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    slide-number: true
    fig-align: center
    menu: false
    include-after-body: assets/no_footer_on_titleslide.html
    footer: "Cail Daley | Astronomy on Tap | April 2026"
    history: false
    hash: true
    pdf-separate-fragments: true
    pdf-max-pages-per-slide: 1
    template-partials:
      - title-slide.html
    center-title-slide: false
image: images/project_cmbeer.png
resources:
  - "images/Lunar-Eclipse-South-Pole.png"
  - "images/spt_observing_clip.mp4"
  - "images/spt_eht_pointing_clip.mp4"
# decktape: decktape reveal --fragments -s 1920x1080 _site/26_AoT_BottomOfTheWorld/26_AoT_BottomOfTheWorld.html 26_AoT_BottomOfTheWorld/26_AoT_BottomOfTheWorld.pdf

html-math-method:
  method: mathjax
  url: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-svg-full.js
include-in-header: assets/mathjax.html
---

## Why can't we do astronomy here?

:::{.paris-flex}
<img class="paris-img" src="images/paris_eiffel_night.jpg">

::: {.paris-bullets}
::: {.incremental}
- Lights
- Clouds
- Humidity
:::
:::
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.paris-flex { display: flex; align-items: stretch; justify-content: center; gap: 2em; height: 880px; }
.paris-flex > p { margin: 0; height: 100%; display: flex; align-items: center; }
.paris-flex .paris-img { height: 100% !important; width: auto !important; max-height: none !important; max-width: none !important; display: block; }
.paris-bullets { font-size: 1.6em; display: flex; align-items: center; }
</style>
```


## Microwaves!

::: {.microwave-wrap}
<img class="microwave-img" src="images/kitchen_microwave.jpg" alt="">
<div class="microwave-credit">Illustration: Harney &amp; Sons Master Tea Blends</div>
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .microwave-wrap { position: relative; display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center; margin-top: 0.3em; }
.reveal .microwave-img { height: 820px !important; width: auto !important; max-height: 820px !important; max-width: 100% !important; display: block; }
.reveal .microwave-credit { position: absolute; bottom: 0.3em; right: 1em; font-size: 0.8em; font-style: italic; color: #555; }
</style>
```

::: {.notes}
Microwaves are light — the same electromagnetic radiation as visible light,
just with wavelengths a million times longer (centimetres instead of nanometres).
Our eyes can't see them, but they're everywhere: wifi, bluetooth, cell phones,
radar, microwave ovens — and the light Lennart just showed you, the cosmic
microwave background, photons left over from 380,000 years after the Big Bang.
Same kind of light, broadly the same part of the spectrum.

**Why a kitchen microwave heats your food.** A magnetron inside the oven emits
at 2.45 GHz. Water is a polar molecule — lopsided charge distribution — so the
oscillating field yanks it back and forth, and friction between molecules
generates heat. (Pedantically: 2.45 GHz isn't a sharp water *resonance*; it's
on the rising shoulder of liquid water's broad Debye relaxation, which peaks
around 10–20 GHz. Water's sharp *rotational* lines sit higher up — 22 and
183 GHz — and live in the atmosphere, where they matter to us. 2.45 GHz was
chosen for ovens because it's an unregulated industrial band that heats food
well and penetrates a few cm deep.)

**The Parkes perytons.** From 1998, the 64-metre radio dish at Parkes in
New South Wales — the same telescope that relayed the Apollo 11 moon-landing
broadcast — started seeing bright millisecond-long bursts. They named them
"perytons," after a mythical antlered deer that casts a human-shaped shadow.
The timing was suspicious: the bursts clustered around lunchtime, almost
always on weekdays. For over a decade no one knew what they were.

It got worse in 2007 when Parkes also detected the first fast radio burst
(FRB) — a millisecond flash from cosmological distances. Perytons looked
close enough to FRBs to threaten the whole young field. Were FRBs even real?

In 2015, Emily Petroff and collaborators (MNRAS 451, 3933) installed an RFI
monitor on-site and cracked it: the culprit was the cafeteria microwave oven.
If a hungry astronomer opened the door *before* the timer hit zero, the
magnetron's shut-down transient leaked a broadband burst that the telescope,
pointed just right, caught as a peryton. Two decades of mysterious signals,
solved by a kitchen appliance.

**The double point.** *First:* cities don't just pollute with visible light —
they scream in microwaves too. Every wifi router, phone, radar, and oven
pumps anthropogenic radiation into the microwave sky. Put a telescope like
SPT in Paris and it would drown in RFI. *Second:* even if you shielded all
that out, the atmosphere itself absorbs at our actual observation bands
(SPT works at 95, 150, 220 GHz) — driven by the water-vapour rotational line
at 183 GHz and its pressure-broadened wings. That's the "humidity" bullet
from the last slide, now with teeth. Going up a mountain — and eventually
to the driest place on Earth — solves both problems at once. Next slide.

(Strictly: microwave ovens themselves aren't a direct problem for CMB
telescopes — 2.45 GHz is ~40–90× below SPT's bands. The Parkes story works
because their multibeam receiver was at 1.4 GHz, and magnetron shut-down
leaks broadband. The real enemy for us is atmospheric water vapour.)
:::


## So we go *up* the mountain:

![Pic du Midi de Bigorre (2877 m). *Credit: Matthieu Pinau*](images/pic_du_midi_clouds.webp){.r-stretch fig-align="center"}

::: {.notes}
Observatories have lived on mountain tops since the 1800s — above the lights,
above the weather, above as much atmosphere as you can climb over.
But clouds aren't even the real enemy.
:::


## The sky isn't transparent

- Even on a perfectly clear day, the air is full of water vapor
- Water vapor absorbs the cosmic microwave background — and, worse, **glows back**

::: {.sky-plot-wrap}
<img class="opacity-plot" src="images/atmospheric_opacity_nasa.png" alt="">
<div class="cmb-box"><span>CMB</span></div>
<div class="sky-credit">Credit: NASA<br>via Wikimedia Commons</div>
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .sky-plot-wrap { position: relative; display: inline-block; max-width: 100%; }
.reveal .opacity-plot { max-height: 738px !important; width: auto !important; max-width: 100% !important; display: block; }
.reveal .cmb-box { position: absolute; left: 61.5%; width: 2.5%; top: 16%; height: 67%; border: 5px solid #e63946; border-radius: 4px; pointer-events: none; }
.reveal .cmb-box > span { position: absolute; top: -1.4em; left: 50%; transform: translateX(-50%); color: #e63946; font-weight: 600; font-size: 0.8em; letter-spacing: 0.05em; }
.reveal .sky-credit { position: absolute; left: calc(100% + 0.5em); bottom: 15%; font-size: 0.7em; line-height: 1.25; text-align: left; font-style: italic; color: #555; white-space: nowrap; }
</style>
```

## Option 1: go to space

- No atmosphere, no weather — can observe everything

::: {.cmb-satellites}
![COBE (1992) · *NASA*](images/cobe_cmb.png){.sat-panel}

![WMAP (2001) · *NASA*](images/wmap_cmb.png){.sat-panel}

![Planck (2009) · *ESA*](images/planck_cmb.png){.sat-panel}
:::

::: {.notes}
**COBE (1989–1993)** — Discovered the cosmic microwave background anisotropies
(tiny one-part-in-100,000 fluctuations — the seeds of all structure). FIRAS also
measured the spectrum as a perfect blackbody at 2.725 K. Nobel Prize 2006 (Mather
& Smoot). Resolution ~7° — too coarse to resolve the acoustic peaks.

**Local hook:** George Smoot — one of the two COBE Nobelists — has been
affiliated with Université Paris Cité since his prize. Worth a mention given
the venue.

**WMAP (2001–2010)** — Measured the acoustic peaks and solidified our current model of
the universe, ΛCDM. Age, composition, flatness — all pinned down precisely.

**Planck (2009–2013)** — Perhaps the most successful cosmology experiment of all time.
Ushered in *precision cosmology*: gold-standard CMB temperature and polarization maps,
CMB lensing, galaxy-cluster catalogues via the SZ effect.

Correction to a common misconception: COBE did NOT detect the first acoustic peak —
that came from balloon experiments around 2000 (BOOMERanG, MAXIMA), then nailed by WMAP.
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .cmb-satellites { display: flex; gap: 0.5em; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; margin-top: 0.5em; width: 100%; }
.reveal .cmb-satellites > .quarto-figure { flex: 1 1 0; text-align: center; margin: 0; }
.reveal .cmb-satellites figure { margin: 0; }
.reveal .cmb-satellites figcaption { text-align: center !important; }
.reveal .cmb-satellites img.sat-panel { max-height: 740px; max-width: 100%; width: auto; display: block; margin: 0 auto; }
</style>
```


## From detection to precision

![](images/cobe_wmap_planck_maps.jpg){.nostretch .precision-img}

*Bigger telescope → sharper map. But rockets are expensive — any other options?*

[*Credit: NASA*]{.precision-credit}

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .precision-img { height: 800px !important; width: auto !important; max-height: none !important; max-width: none !important; display: block; margin: 0 auto; }
.reveal .precision-credit { display: block; text-align: center; font-size: 0.5em; color: #888; margin-top: 0.4em; }
</style>
```

::: {.notes}
The iconic progression: CMB sky as measured by COBE (blurry), WMAP (sharper), Planck
(razor-sharp). One part in 100,000 — colour scale pushed to the limit. In ~20 years
we went from *seeing* structure to *measuring* it precisely enough to pin down the
age, geometry, composition, and expansion of the universe.
:::


## {.no-title}

![](images/project_cmbeer.png){.nostretch .cmbeer-img}

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal section.no-title h2 { display: none; }
.reveal .cmbeer-img { height: 950px !important; width: auto !important; max-height: none !important; max-width: 100% !important; display: block; margin: 0 auto; }
</style>
```


## Option 2: fly a balloon

- Rise above ~99% of the atmosphere — almost as good as space

::: {.balloon-row}
<iframe class="balloon-video" src="https://www.youtube-nocookie.com/embed/hK_kWN0BsKs?rel=0&autoplay=1&mute=1&loop=1&playlist=hK_kWN0BsKs&playsinline=1" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen></iframe>

::: {.balloon-col}
<img class="balloon-path" src="images/anita_flight_path.png" alt="">
<div class="balloon-credit">ANITA-IV flight path. <em>Gorham et al. 2019</em></div>
:::
:::

*Cheap, but you can only fly for weeks and the balloon has to land somewhere.*

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .balloon-row { display: flex; gap: 1em; align-items: center; justify-content: center; margin-top: 0.5em; }
.reveal .balloon-video { flex: 0 0 62%; aspect-ratio: 16/9; border: 0; display: block; }
.reveal .balloon-row > p { margin: 0; }
.reveal .balloon-col { flex: 0 0 36%; }
.reveal .balloon-path { width: 100% !important; max-width: 100% !important; max-height: none !important; height: auto !important; display: block; }
.reveal .balloon-credit { font-size: 0.55em; color: #555; text-align: center; margin-top: 0.3em; }
</style>
```

::: {.notes}
This is ANITA-IV — a neutrino balloon experiment, not CMB, but the flight path is
the perfect illustration: three full loops around Antarctica over 28 days before
landing near the South Pole. Cheap, but this is why balloons aren't a permanent solution.
Fig. 3 from Gorham et al., arXiv:1902.04005.
:::


## Option 3: stay on the ground

- Ground-based telescopes can be enormous, run for years, be upgraded

::: {.dry-question}
*Where on Earth is dry enough?*
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.dry-row}

::: {.dry-place}
<img class="dry-photo" src="images/atacama_simons.jpg" alt="">

<p><strong>Atacama Desert, Chile</strong><br>5000 m · −20 °C to +10 °C<br>&lt; 20 mm precipitation / year</p>
:::

::: {.dry-place}
<img class="dry-photo" src="images/spt_moon.jpg" alt="">

<p><strong>Antarctic Plateau</strong><br>2835 m · −60 °C to −25 °C<br>&lt; 3 mm precipitation / year</p>
:::

:::
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .dry-question { text-align: center; margin: 0.4em 0 0.3em; font-size: 1.1em; }
.reveal .dry-row { display: flex; gap: 1.5em; align-items: flex-start; justify-content: center; }
.reveal .dry-place { flex: 0 0 48%; text-align: center; }
.reveal .dry-photo { width: 100%; height: 460px; object-fit: cover; display: block; max-width: 100% !important; max-height: none !important; }
.reveal .dry-place p { margin-top: 0.4em; font-size: 0.9em; line-height: 1.4; }
</style>
```

::: {.notes}
Two mountainous deserts. Atacama is the obvious one; Antarctica is the surprise —
by area it's actually the largest desert on Earth. The Antarctic Plateau is lower
than Chajnantor but far drier.

Credits: Atacama — Deborah Kellner (Simons Observatory). South Pole — SPT collaboration.
:::


## Getting to the bottom of the world

:::: {.bottom-2col}

::: {.bottom-text-col}
- The South Pole is only reachable by plane, and only during austral summer (October–February)
- Christchurch<br>→ McMurdo<br>→ South Pole
- Once winter closes in around February, you're frozen in until October

*About 40 people spend the winter there.*
:::

::: {.bottom-map-col}
<img class="bottom-map-img" src="images/antarctica_route_map.png" alt="">
:::

::::

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .bottom-2col { display: flex; gap: 1.5em; align-items: center; margin-top: 0.3em; height: 820px; }
.reveal .bottom-text-col { flex: 0 0 50%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center; }
.reveal .bottom-map-col { flex: 0 0 48%; height: 100%; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; }
.reveal .bottom-map-img { width: 100% !important; height: 100% !important; max-width: 100% !important; max-height: 100% !important; object-fit: contain; display: block; }
</style>
```


## McMurdo: landing on sea ice

![](images/mcmurdo_c130_seaice.jpg){.nostretch .mcmurdo-img}

[*Photo: Alex Polak*]{.mcmurdo-credit}

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .mcmurdo-img { height: 900px !important; width: auto !important; max-height: none !important; max-width: 100% !important; display: block; margin: 0.3em auto 0; }
.reveal .mcmurdo-credit { position: absolute; bottom: 16px; right: 24px; color: white; font-size: 0.45em; opacity: 0.85; text-shadow: 0 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,0.7); }
</style>
```

::: {.notes}
That plane is an LC-130 Hercules — the ski-equipped C-130, operated by the US
109th Airlift Wing. It's the only aircraft that can land at the South Pole proper.
Here it's on the McMurdo sea-ice runway with a marshaller in red ECW gear.
:::


## Big planes.

::: {.planes-row}
::: {.plane-panel}
<img class="plane-img" src="images/c17_exterior.jpg" alt="">
<div class="plane-cap">C-17 Globemaster III<br><span class="plane-credit">*Photo: Melanie Archipley*</span></div>
:::

::: {.plane-panel}
<img class="plane-img" src="images/c17_interior.jpg" alt="">
<div class="plane-cap">inside the C-17<br><span class="plane-credit">*Photo: Melanie Archipley*</span></div>
:::
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .planes-row { display: flex; gap: 0.4em; align-items: center; justify-content: center; margin-top: 0.5em; }
.reveal .plane-panel { text-align: center; }
.reveal .plane-img { height: 740px !important; width: auto !important; max-width: none !important; max-height: 740px !important; display: block; margin: 0 auto; }
.reveal .plane-cap { text-align: center; margin-top: 0.3em; font-size: 0.8em; font-style: italic; color: #444; }
.reveal .plane-credit { font-size: 0.75em; color: #666; }
</style>
```


## The South Pole Telescope

:::: {.spt-2col}

::: {.spt-text-col}

::: {.spt-tagline}
*continuous night, for months*<br>
*the coldest, driest air on Earth*
:::

- since 2007
- 16,000 pixels
- 10 m — 6× the size of space CMB telescopes
:::

::: {.spt-photo-col}
<img class="spt-hero-img" src="images/spt_halo.jpg" alt="">
<div class="spt-credit-col">Photo: Aman Chokshi</div>
:::

::::

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .spt-2col { display: flex; gap: 1.5em; align-items: center; margin-top: 0.3em; height: 820px; }
.reveal .spt-text-col { flex: 0 0 58%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center; }
.reveal .spt-tagline { font-size: 1.15em; line-height: 1.5; margin-bottom: 1.5em; }
.reveal .spt-photo-col { flex: 0 0 38%; position: relative; height: 100%; }
.reveal .spt-hero-img { width: 100% !important; height: 100% !important; max-width: 100% !important; max-height: 100% !important; object-fit: contain; display: block; }
.reveal .spt-credit-col { position: absolute; bottom: 6px; right: 10px; color: white; font-size: 0.45em; opacity: 0.75; text-shadow: 0 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,0.7); }
</style>
```

::: {.notes}
Months of continuous night means stable observing: no day-night thermal cycles,
no sun glare, a sky that just sits still. And the cold — about −60 °C in winter —
means the air holds almost no water vapor, which is the real atmospheric enemy.
High and dry we already established; here we add dark and cold.

The telescope has carried three cameras over about 20 years, each one more
sensitive. SPT-3G has 16× more pixels than the original SPT-SZ and three times
the frequency coverage. It's still running.
:::


## The SPT-3G camera

:::: {.camera-2col}

::: {.camera-panel}
<img class="camera-img" src="images/spt3g_team.jpg" alt="">
<div class="camera-cap">camera & commissioning team, 2017</div>
:::

::: {.camera-panel}
<img class="camera-img" src="images/spt3g_pixel.png" alt="">
<div class="camera-cap">a SPT-3G pixel<br><span class="camera-credit">*Credit: Adam Anderson*</span></div>
:::

::::

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .camera-2col { display: flex; gap: 2em; align-items: center; justify-content: center; margin-top: 0.5em; height: 820px; }
.reveal .camera-panel { flex: 0 0 48%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; justify-content: center; height: 100%; }
.reveal .camera-img { max-height: 760px !important; max-width: 100% !important; width: auto !important; height: auto !important; object-fit: contain; display: block; }
.reveal .camera-cap { text-align: center; margin-top: 0.4em; font-size: 0.85em; line-height: 1.3; }
.reveal .camera-credit { font-size: 0.75em; color: #666; }
</style>
```


## SPT observes by scanning

<video class="obs-video" src="images/spt_observing_clip.mp4" autoplay loop muted playsinline></video>

[*Video: Aman Chokshi*]{.obs-credit}

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .obs-video { display: block; margin: 0.3em auto 0; height: 920px; width: auto; aspect-ratio: 16/9; object-fit: cover; max-width: 100%; max-height: 920px; background: transparent; }
.reveal .obs-credit { position: absolute; bottom: 16px; right: 24px; color: white; font-size: 0.45em; opacity: 0.85; text-shadow: 0 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,0.7); }
</style>
```

::: {.notes}
The telescope rocks back and forth across the same patch of southern sky, hour
after hour. Scanning modulates the sky signal above the atmosphere's and
detectors' low-frequency drift noise. Thousands of hours of data combine into
a single deep map.
:::


## Event Horizon Telescope calibration

<video class="obs-video" src="images/spt_eht_pointing_clip.mp4" autoplay loop muted playsinline></video>

[*Video: Aman Chokshi*]{.obs-credit}

::: {.notes}
SPT participates in the Event Horizon Telescope — the global VLBI network that
imaged the black holes at M87 and the Galactic Center. This clip shows a pointing
calibration sequence.
:::


## SPT sees everything

::: {.sees-row}
::: {.sees-panel}
<img class="sees-img" src="images/foster_fig10_starlink_150ghz.png" alt="">
<div class="sees-cap">Starlink constellation<br><span class="sees-sub">*Credit: Allen Foster*</span></div>
:::

::: {.sees-panel}
<img class="sees-img" src="images/foster_fig6_cobe_150ghz.png" alt="">
<div class="sees-cap">COBE (detection)<br><span class="sees-sub">*Credit: Allen Foster*</span></div>
:::

::: {.sees-panel}
<img class="sees-img" src="images/cobe_cmb.png" alt="">
<div class="sees-cap">COBE (spacecraft)<br><span class="sees-sub">*Credit: NASA*</span></div>
:::
:::

::: {.sees-tagline}
*We've come a long way from the first blurry COBE maps in 1992.*
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .sees-row { display: flex; gap: 1.5em; align-items: center; justify-content: center; margin-top: 0.3em; }
.reveal .sees-panel { flex: 1 1 0; text-align: center; max-width: 33%; }
.reveal .sees-img { height: 640px !important; max-height: 640px !important; max-width: 100% !important; width: auto !important; object-fit: contain; display: block; margin: 0 auto; }
.reveal .sees-cap { text-align: center; margin-top: 0.4em; font-size: 0.85em; font-style: italic; color: #444; line-height: 1.3; }
.reveal .sees-sub { font-size: 0.75em; color: #666; }
.reveal .sees-tagline { text-align: center; margin-top: 1em; font-size: 1.15em; }
</style>
```


##  {data-background="images/Lunar-Eclipse-South-Pole.png" data-background-size="cover" data-background-position="center" footer="false"}

[Thank you!]{.thankyou-text}

[*Photo: Aman Chokshi*]{.thankyou-credit}

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .thankyou-text { display: block; color: white; font-size: 2.2em; font-weight: 500; text-align: center; margin-top: 900px; text-shadow: 0 2px 12px rgba(0,0,0,0.85); }
.reveal .thankyou-credit { position: absolute; bottom: 20px; right: 40px; color: white; font-size: 0.4em; opacity: 0.7; }
</style>
```


## Shrines {visibility="uncounted"}

::: {.bonus-row}
<img class="bonus-img" src="images/bonus_shrine_bottles.jpg" alt="">

<img class="bonus-img" src="images/bonus_shrine_vanilla.jpg" alt="">
:::


## New Year's {visibility="uncounted"}

![](images/bonus_new_year.jpg){.nostretch .bonus-solo}


## {footer="false" visibility="uncounted"}

![](images/bonus_spt_dish_people.jpg){.nostretch .bonus-solo}


## Telescope maintenance {visibility="uncounted"}

::: {.bonus-row}
<img class="bonus-img" src="images/bonus_maintenance_grease.jpg" alt="">

<img class="bonus-img" src="images/bonus_maintenance_dish.jpg" alt="">
:::


## {footer="false" visibility="uncounted"}

::: {.bonus-row}
<img class="bonus-img" src="images/bonus_lift_cage.jpg" alt="">

<img class="bonus-img" src="images/bonus_lift_crane.jpg" alt="">
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .bonus-row { display: flex; gap: 1em; align-items: center; justify-content: center; margin-top: 0.3em; }
.reveal .bonus-row > p { margin: 0; }
.reveal .bonus-img { height: 820px !important; width: auto !important; max-height: 820px !important; max-width: 100% !important; object-fit: contain; display: block; }
.reveal .bonus-solo { height: 920px !important; width: auto !important; max-height: 920px !important; max-width: 100% !important; object-fit: contain; display: block; margin: 0.2em auto 0; }
</style>
```

::: {.notes}
Bonus slides — all photos from Melanie Archipley's winter-over deck.
:::
